title: Fixing a sticking door
subtitle: Find where it binds, plane or pack, rehang
excerpt: How to diagnose and fix a door that sticks in its frame: identifying where the door binds by reading the paint rub mark, establishing whether the frame has moved or the door has swollen, and correcting the problem by planing, adjusting hinges, or packing the frame.
sourceNotes: Standard UK carpentry diagnostic practice. Collins Complete DIY Manual. BSI guidance on door fitting tolerances from BS 8214.

recipeTools.notes: For planing a swollen or out-of-square door edge.
For light passes on end grain and narrow areas.
For checking where the door stands proud of the frame.
For re-drilling hinge screw holes if screws are stripped.

aliases: how to fix a sticking door

content.text: A door sticks for one of four reasons: the timber has swollen with humidity; the frame has moved; the hinge screws have pulled loose and the door has sagged; or paint build-up has reduced the clearance. Diagnosing which before reaching for the plane saves unnecessary work.
Diagnosis
Step 1, find where the door binds.

Open and close the door slowly and feel exactly where it drags. Rub a piece of chalk or soft pencil along the door stop and the door edge, then close the door; the chalk transfers to the door at the point of contact. Note whether the bind is at the top, bottom, or latch side.
Step 2, check the hinges.

Inspect the hinge screws: if any are loose or spinning, tighten them first. A door that sags on loose hinges binds at the top of the latch side; once the hinges are tight this bind may resolve without any planing. If the screw holes are stripped, remove the screws, pack the holes with glued wooden matchsticks or short lengths of dowel, allow to cure, and refit the screws.
Paint build-up
Step 3, check for paint build-up.

Each coat of paint adds about 0.05 mm to the door face. After 10 or 15 redecorations, the clearance gap can be completely closed. If the door bind started after a recent repaint, try a quick pass with a sharp block plane or coarse sandpaper along the hinge-side edge first.
Planing
Step 4, remove the door to plane it.

Remove the hinge-pin (tap upward with a screwdriver and hammer for loose-pin butt hinges; unscrew for fixed-pin types). Lift the door clear and prop it with a door wedge or workmate. Plane the binding edge using a sharp plane set to a fine cut. Plane along the grain on the stile edges; plane across from the corners toward the centre on the top and bottom edges to avoid splitting.
Step 5, check and rehang.

Offer the door back into the frame and check the clearance gap. It should be consistent: about 2 mm at the top and latch sides, about 3 mm at the bottom over a hard floor (8 mm if carpet is to be fitted). Rehang on the hinges and swing the door several times to confirm the bind is resolved.
Step 6, seal the planed edges.
Bare timber edges absorb moisture and can re-swell. Prime and paint or seal any planed edges before rehanging. This step is especially important on external doors and in humid rooms.
Frame movement

If the door has been hanging and closing correctly for years and has only recently started sticking, and there is no sign of timber swelling or hinge failure, the frame may have moved. This is more common in older houses. The floor may have settled, or the lintel above the door may have deflected. If the door frame itself is visibly out of square, the problem is structural and should be investigated before any cosmetic repair.
Common mistakes
Planing too much and opening the clearance gap too wide.

The door sticks in summer and rattles in winter because timber moves seasonally. Check the door in winter before removing material; a door that sticks slightly in a hot summer may need no intervention at all.
